did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a lightweight deep learning model (OSDL) with a novel Apex Component Analysis Network (ACAN) for skin cancer classification. It employs the Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm (SFLA) to optimize hyperparameters and processes dermoscopy images from the ISBI-2016 dataset to classify melanoma versus benign lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, specifically proposing a new lightweight deep learning model (OSDL) with an Apex Component Analysis Network (ACAN) optimized by the Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm (SFLA). It details the methodology, dataset (ISBI-2016), implementation, and provides quantitative results (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score) comparing the proposed model against existing methods. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICDSNS), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Detection of Skin Cancer using SFLA based Apex Component Analysis Network
journal: 2023 International Conference on Data Science and Network Security (ICDSNS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification with limited training data by proposing a lightweight deep learning model called OSDL, enhanced by a novel Apex Component Analysis Network (ACAN). The Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm (SFLA) is employed to optimize the hyperparameters of the OSDL model. The research utilizes the publicly available ISBI-2016 dataset, splitting 900 labeled dermoscopy images into training and testing sets, with data augmentation applied to handle class imbalance. The model's performance was evaluated against existing techniques such as CNN, SVM, RNN, and MFO-Fuzzy networks using metrics including accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score. The proposed SFLA-OSDL model achieved superior results, reaching 98.35% accuracy, 98.33% recall, 97.72% precision, and 97.27% F1-measure when trained on 80% of the data. These findings demonstrate that the lightweight architecture effectively improves feature expression and classification accuracy even with scarce training samples.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task on the ISBI-2016 dataset. The study used 243 melanoma images for training and 30 for testing (before augmentation).
The comparator class in the binary classification task on the ISBI-2016 dataset. The study used 490 benign lesion images for training and 137 for testing (before augmentation).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Melanoma vs. Benign skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin cancer using the ISBI-2016 dataset. The model predicts discrete labels for melanoma versus benign lesions. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score, with the proposed SFLA-OSDL model achieving 98.35% accuracy on the test set.

datasets_used.name: ISBI-2016
datasets_used.description: A subset of the larger ISIC dataset containing labeled dermoscopy images of skin lesions. The study used the 900 labeled images from the training set of this subset.

use.training: Used for training the SFLA-OSDL model. The initial 765 images were augmented (geometric alterations, flipping) to increase the dataset size to 1952 images (972 melanoma, 980 benign) to handle class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance. The remaining 167 images (15% of the 900 labeled images) were used as the test set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed SFLA-OSDL model and existing models (AE, SVM, RNN, CNN, MFO-Fuzzy U-net) were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-measure) without statistical significance testing.

70% Training / 30% Testing Split.notes: The proposed model achieved the highest performance across all metrics in this split. AE showed the lowest precision (73.15%) and F1-measure (80.30%). CNN had the lowest recall (65.47%).

80% Training / 20% Testing Split.notes: With increased training data, the proposed model achieved superior results: 98.35% accuracy, 98.33% recall, 97.72% precision, and 97.27% F1-measure. AE continued to show lower precision (68.39%) and F1-measure (75.09%). The MFO-Fuzzy U-net showed strong performance with 96.92% accuracy and 94.75% recall.

architecture_used.main_model: SFLA-OSDL (Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm optimized Optimized Simple Deep Learning model)
architecture_used.pretraining: None reported; the model is built from scratch using intrinsic features of the skin cancer images.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (images resized to this dimension for processing).

architecture_details.layers: Four layers total: Input layer, two convolution layers, and an output layer.
architecture_details.input_layer: Uses a Residual Gradient Function (RGF) to merge spatial and spectral data.
architecture_details.convolution_layers: Kernels are derived from ACA-smoothed picture data (endmembers extracted via Apex Component Analysis) rather than standard PCA sliding patches. Dimensionality reduction is applied to the endmembers before forming the kernels.
architecture_details.output_layer: Calculates feature expression for each pixel.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Simplified Deep Learning (SDL) network with a regularization value set to 1.

training_details.optimizer: Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm (SFLA) used for hyper-parameter tuning of the SDL network.
training_details.hyperparameters: Optimized via SFLA; specific values not reported other than SDL regularization value of 1.
training_details.training_schedule: Trained on augmented dataset of 1952 images (derived from 900 original ISBI-2016 training images). Evaluated on 167 test images.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported; described as 'lightweight' and 'substantially simpler' than standard convolutional deep learning models.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SFLA-OSDL (Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm optimized Optimized Simple Deep Learning model)
MFO-Fuzzy U-net (May Fly Optimizer Fuzzy U-net)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Training photos were enlarged to 224x224 pixels.
Data Imaging: Conversion of pixel spectra into images to maintain physical properties and allow texture to represent spectral disparities.
Bilateral Filtering (BF): Applied to remove distracting noise artifacts; combines spatial and intensity filtering using Gaussian filters based on intensity and physical distance.
Image Augmentation: Geometric alterations, vertical and horizontal alignment switching, and flipping at an angle to handle class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
Dimensionality Reduction: Applied to endmembers derived from Apex Component Analysis (ACA) before forming convolution kernels.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training photos to handle class imbalance and prevent overfitting. Specifically, vertical and horizontal alignments were switched, and images were flipped at an angle.
Training photos were enlarged to 224x224 pixels prior to augmentation.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic Data Generation via Sub-image Extraction
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Melanoma images were enhanced to produce four sub-images each, increasing the count from 243 to 972. Benign tissue images were supplemented to 980. The total augmented training set size was 1952 images.

conclusions: The proposed SFLA-OSDL model, which combines a lightweight Optimized Simple Deep Learning (OSDL) architecture with an Apex Component Analysis Network (ACAN) and hyperparameter optimization via the Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm (SFLA), effectively addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification with limited training data.

The model demonstrated superior performance compared to existing methods (AE, SVM, RNN, CNN, and MFO-Fuzzy U-net) on the ISBI-2016 dataset, achieving 98.35% accuracy, 98.33% recall, 97.72% precision, and 97.27% F1-measure with an 80% training/20% testing split.

The use of intrinsic image features (spatial contextual information and spectral characteristics) via the ACAN allows for robust feature expression even with a scarcity of training samples, outperforming standard deep learning models in terms of accuracy and speed.
While the proposed model is time-consuming during training, its architecture is substantially simpler than standard convolutional deep learning models.
The authors acknowledge that future work will focus on further streamlining the network architecture while simultaneously boosting classification precision.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Benign lesions) using only 900 labeled images from the ISBI-2016 dataset, which restricts the generalizability to multi-class skin cancer types or broader dermatological contexts.

The dataset is highly imbalanced (490 benign vs. 243 melanoma images in the training set), necessitating aggressive data augmentation (increasing training samples to 1952) to prevent model bias and overfitting.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons against baseline models (AE, SVM, RNN, CNN, MFO-Fuzzy U-net).
The proposed SFLA-OSDL model is described as 'time-consuming' during the training phase, which may impact its practical deployment efficiency despite its lightweight architecture.
Hyperparameter optimization was performed using the Shuffled Frog Leaping Algorithm (SFLA), but specific optimized hyperparameter values are not explicitly reported in the text.

The model was evaluated on a single public dataset (ISBI-2016) without external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of robustness across different imaging devices or populations.
The paper acknowledges that future work is needed to further streamline the network architecture and boost classification precision, indicating current limitations in architectural efficiency.
